summary: Gruber revisits his earlier characterization of the Flashback Mac malware outbreak as 'not an epidemic' after Ed Bott challenged him on Twitter. He examines the CDC's definition of epidemic — more cases than expected — and applies it to the 600,000 infected Macs, roughly 1% of the active installed base. Crucially, he discovers that this percentage actually exceeds the worst-ever Windows malware outbreak (Conficker at 0.7%), which forces him to concede he was wrong. He admits 'epidemic' is the right word while maintaining it's not cause for hysteria.
key_points: The Flashback malware infected roughly 600,000 Macs, about 1% of the total active installed base
By percentage, Flashback exceeded the largest-ever Windows infection (Conficker at 0.7%), undermining the assumption that Mac malware is comparatively minor
Gruber's initial resistance stemmed from incorrectly assuming the worst Windows outbreaks infected a much higher percentage of PCs
The CDC's definition of epidemic — more cases than expected — applies neatly to Mac malware, where any significant outbreak exceeds expectations
money_quotes: It's not an epidemic, but it's definitely real, and insidious.
Computer malware and human disease are an apples-to-oranges comparison if I've ever seen one, but that definition from the CDC strikes me as apt for malware.
I'd say a Mac malware outbreak that is more common, on a percentage basis, than the largest-ever Windows infection is without question more cases than expected.
I was wrong: epidemic is indeed the right word.
Cause for hysteria? No. But an epidemic? Yes.

My initial resistance to describing Flashback as an 'epidemic' was largely because I had assumed, incorrectly, that the worst Windows malware outbreaks infected far more than 0.7 percent of the PC installed base.
It turns out one percent is record-breaking bad.
key_insight: Gruber publicly admits he was wrong about Flashback not being an epidemic, after discovering that its 1% infection rate actually exceeded the worst-ever Windows malware outbreak on a percentage basis.
